<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/skills/xero-auth/scripts/token-cache.ts`

```text
1	// .claude/skills/xero-auth/scripts/token-cache.ts
2	//
3	// In-memory client_credentials token cache for a Xero Custom Connection.
4	// Copy into api/src/modules/xero/ and register as a NestJS provider via
5	// useFactory (see SKILL.md) — this file itself has zero framework imports so
6	// it compiles standalone under strict / ES2022 / commonjs / types=["node"].
7	//
8	// Why this exists: Xero's own MCP server requests a brand-new token (plus a
9	// GET /connections) on EVERY call — two wasted round-trips per call, all
10	// counted against the 60/min tenant rate limit. Do the opposite:
11	//   - cache the token in memory until expiry minus a 60s buffer
12	//     (pattern: xero-command-line TOKEN_BUFFER_MS = 60_000),
13	//   - single-flight concurrent token requests,
14	//   - on 401 invalidate + fetch a fresh token + retry exactly once
15	//     (pattern: xero-command-line withRetry).
16	// client_credentials issues NO refresh_token (response fields: access_token,
17	// expires_in, token_type, scope) — "refresh" simply means asking again.
18	
19	const XERO_TOKEN_URL = "https://identity.xero.com/connect/token";
20	
21	/** Refresh this long before expiry, so in-flight calls never race the deadline. */
22	const EXPIRY_BUFFER_MS = 60_000;
23	
24	export class MissingCredentialError extends Error {
25	  constructor(name: string) {
26	    super(
27	      `${name} is empty — if it reaches Xero the token endpoint answers a misleading ` +
28	        `400 invalid_request, which points away from the real cause. ` +
29	        `Set it in .env and fail at boot, not mid-request.`,
30	    );
31	    this.name = "MissingCredential";
32	  }
33	}
34	
35	export class XeroTokenError extends Error {
36	  constructor(
37	    public readonly status: number,
38	    public readonly body: string,
39	  ) {
40	    super(`Xero token request failed with HTTP ${status}: ${body}`);
41	    this.name = "XeroTokenError";
42	  }
43	}
44	
45	export interface XeroTokenCacheConfig {
46	  clientId: string;
47	  clientSecret: string;
48	  /** Space-separated scope list, sent in the token request body. */
49	  scope: string;
50	}
51	
52	export class XeroTokenCache {
53	  private accessToken: string | null = null;
54	  private expiresAtMs = 0;
55	  private inflight: Promise<string> | null = null;
56	
57	  constructor(private readonly config: XeroTokenCacheConfig) {
58	    if (config.clientId.trim() === "") throw new MissingCredentialError("XERO_CLIENT_ID");
59	    if (config.clientSecret.trim() === "") throw new MissingCredentialError("XERO_CLIENT_SECRET");
60	    if (config.scope.trim() === "") throw new MissingCredentialError("scope");
61	  }
62	
63	  /** Returns a valid access token, reusing the cached one when fresh. */
64	  async getAccessToken(): Promise<string> {
65	    if (this.accessToken !== null && Date.now() < this.expiresAtMs - EXPIRY_BUFFER_MS) {
66	      return this.accessToken;
67	    }
68	    // Single-flight: concurrent callers share one token request instead of
69	    // each burning a round-trip against the rate limit.
70	    if (this.inflight === null) {
71	      this.inflight = this.requestToken().finally(() => {
72	        this.inflight = null;
73	      });
74	    }
75	    return this.inflight;
76	  }
77	
78	  /** Drop the cached token (call on a 401 before retrying). */
79	  invalidate(): void {
80	    this.accessToken = null;
81	    this.expiresAtMs = 0;
82	  }
83	
84	  private async requestToken(): Promise<string> {
85	    const basic = Buffer.from(`${this.config.clientId}:${this.config.clientSecret}`).toString(
86	      "base64",
87	    );
88	    const res = await fetch(XERO_TOKEN_URL, {
89	      method: "POST",
90	      headers: {
91	        Authorization: `Basic ${basic}`,
92	        // Form-encoded, NEVER JSON — the token endpoint is not a JSON API.
93	        "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
94	        Accept: "application/json",
95	      },
96	      body: `grant_type=client_credentials&scope=${encodeURIComponent(this.config.scope)}`,
97	    });
98	    const text = await res.text();
99	    if (!res.ok) {
100	      // Body carries Xero's OAuth error (unauthorized_client, invalid_scope, ...)
101	      // and never contains the secret, so it is safe to surface.
102	      throw new XeroTokenError(res.status, text);
103	    }
104	    const parsed = JSON.parse(text) as { access_token?: unknown; expires_in?: unknown };
105	    if (typeof parsed.access_token !== "string" || parsed.access_token === "") {
106	      throw new XeroTokenError(res.status, "response body had no access_token");
107	    }
108	    const expiresInSec = typeof parsed.expires_in === "number" ? parsed.expires_in : 0;
109	    this.accessToken = parsed.access_token;
110	    this.expiresAtMs = Date.now() + expiresInSec * 1000;
111	    return this.accessToken;
112	  }
113	}
114	
115	export interface XeroFetchInit {
116	  method?: string;
117	  headers?: Record<string, string>;
118	  body?: string;
119	}
120	
121	/**
122	 * fetch against the Xero API with the cached token; on 401, invalidate,
123	 * fetch a fresh token, and retry exactly once. 403 is NOT retried — that is
124	 * a missing scope, and the same token can never succeed.
125	 *
126	 * No xero-tenant-id header: a Custom Connection is bound to a single
127	 * organisation and the documented call sends only Authorization + Accept.
128	 */
129	export async function xeroFetch(
130	  cache: XeroTokenCache,
131	  url: string,
132	  init?: XeroFetchInit,
133	): Promise<Response> {
134	  const doFetch = async (): Promise<Response> => {
135	    const token = await cache.getAccessToken();
136	    return fetch(url, {
137	      method: init?.method ?? "GET",
138	      headers: {
139	        Accept: "application/json",
140	        ...(init?.headers ?? {}),
141	        Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
142	      },
143	      body: init?.body,
144	    });
145	  };
146	
147	  const first = await doFetch();
148	  if (first.status !== 401) {
149	    return first;
150	  }
151	  cache.invalidate();
152	  return doFetch();
153	}
154
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# Xero Auth + API Conventions — Verified Fact Sheet
2	
3	Verified 2026-07-04 against the live official docs at developer.xero.com. Pages are client-side rendered (plain HTTP fetch returns an empty shell), so each page was rendered in Chrome and the visible text extracted. Every fact below is tagged with the source URL. Facts that could not be confirmed on the fetched pages are marked UNVERIFIED.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Custom Connections
8	
9	Source: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/custom-connections/
10	
11	- **Token endpoint**: `POST https://identity.xero.com/connect/token`
12	- **Auth on the token request**: `Authorization: "Basic " + base64encode(client_id + ":" + client_secret)` with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`
13	- **grant_type**: `client_credentials` — "The request body will need to contain the grant type (client_credentials) and scope"
14	- **Scopes — configured at BOTH ends**:
15	  - At app creation: "Next you'll need to select the API scopes your integration will need and who will authorise the connection." (step 2)
16	  - In the token request body: `scope=A space separated list of the scopes required`
17	  - The Client Credentials guide adds: "Note: this differs from the Code and PKCE grant types where scopes are requested as parameters in the URL." (https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/client-credentials/)
18	  - Scope changes coming: "From 29 April 2026, we're changing how scopes work for custom connections... All custom connections will have access to granular scopes... New custom connections will no longer have access to the `accounting.journals.read` scope... If you have an existing custom connection, you can continue using broad scopes until September 2027."
19	- **Token response fields**: `access_token`, `expires_in` ("The amount of seconds until the access token expires"), `token_type: Bearer`, `scope`. The access token is a JWT.
20	- **Token lifetime**: The custom-connections page itself gives no number (its example JWT has `nbf: 1589363023`, `exp: 1589364823` = 1800 s). The explicit statement is on the Token types page: "They are only valid for up to 30 minutes" — https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/token-types/
21	- **Bound to ONE organisation** (exact quote): "Custom Connections are a premium integration option that utilise the client credentials grant type to access data from a single Xero organisation. If your app needs to connect to multiple Xero organisations then you should use the code flow or PKCE flow instead."
22	- **Region restriction**: "Custom Connections are only available for Xero organisations in Australia, New Zealand, the UK, and the US and require an additional monthly subscription." Free connection to the Xero Demo Company for development; Custom Connection apps do not count toward the two-uncertified-app limit.
23	- **xero-tenant-id header**: UNVERIFIED as an explicit statement — the current custom-connections page never mentions the header. What the page does show (step 7, "Call the Xero API"): "Make calls against the Xero APIs by simply adding the following headers to your request:" followed by an example containing ONLY two headers:
24	  ```
25	  GET ../api.xro/2.0/Invoices
26	  Authorization: "Bearer " + access_token
27	  Accept: application/json
28	  ```
29	  i.e. no `xero-tenant-id` header in the documented call. Contrast with standard (code-flow) tokens, where the Token types page requires it: "Make calls against the Xero APIs by simply adding the following headers to your request: `Authorization: "Bearer " + access_token`; `xero-tenant-id: The tenantId context for the request.`" with example `xero-tenant-id: 45e4708e-d862-4111-ab3a-dd8cd03913e1` (https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/token-types/). So the header is omitted from the custom-connection call example, but the sentence "the xero-tenant-id header is not required" does not appear on the current page.
30	- Related distinction (https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/client-credentials/): "Custom Connection apps use a modified version of client credentials which is not described on this page. They can only access tenanted endpoints and cannot access non-tenanted endpoints." Plain client-credentials tokens are the opposite: non-tenanted endpoints only.
31	
32	---
33	
34	## 2. Rate limits
35	
36	Source: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/limits/
37	
38	- **Per tenant** ("organisation, account or practice"):
39	  - Concurrent Limit: **5 calls in progress at one time**
40	  - Minute Limit: **60 calls per minute**
41	  - Daily Limit: **1,000 calls per day for starter, 5,000 calls per day for higher tiers**
42	- **App-wide**: "There is also a limit to the number of API calls your app can make per minute across all tenants. App Minute Limit: **10,000 calls per minute**"
43	- **Quota headers** (exact quote): "Each API response you receive will include the `X-DayLimit-Remaining`, `X-MinLimit-Remaining` and `X-AppMinLimit-Remaining` headers telling you the number of remaining against each limit."
44	- **429 behaviour** (exact quote): "Exceeding a rate limit will result in an HTTP 429 (too many requests) response. It will include an `X-Rate-Limit-Problem` header telling you which limit you have reached."
45	- **Retry-After** (exact quote): "If you have exceed the minute or daily limit you will also receive a `Retry-After` http header that tells you how many seconds to wait before making another request. Requests are counted against a fixed window which will reset at different times for each tenant. It is important to use the Retry-After header to know when you can start making calls again."
46	- **Limits are per tenant, not shared**: "the limits are per tenant. For example if two separate Xero organisations are connected to your application, each connection would have 5000 API calls available in a given 24 hour period for apps in the Core tier and above and 1000 API calls available for apps in the Starter tier."
47	- **Request size limit**: "The maximum request size limit for all APIs is 10MB." Recommended batching: "bundles of up to 50" elements. (Elsewhere on the page: "a ceiling of about 50 nodes per request is practical – this will ensure a request does not exceed the maximum size of 3.5MB.")
48	- **Uncertified app limits**: "each organisation or practice is limited to connecting a maximum of two uncertified apps." New apps default to the starter tier with 5 connections; Core gives up to 50 connections.
49	
50	---
51	
52	## 3. Accounting API request conventions
53	
54	Source: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/requests-and-responses
55	
56	- **Base URL / format**: `https://api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0/{Resource}`. "By default all successful responses on the accounting API are returned as XML." JSON via `Accept: application/json`. Individual Invoices, Quotes and Credit Notes can be returned as PDF via `Accept: application/pdf`.
57	- **JSON date format**: Microsoft .NET style, e.g. `"DateTimeUTC": "\/Date(1439434356790)\/"` — "a unix timestamp value, but in miliseconds rather than seconds".
58	
59	### Pagination
60	- Endpoints (exact quote): "Paged results are available on the **Invoices, Contacts, CreditNotes, BankTransactions, ManualJournals, Payments, PurchaseOrders, Prepayments and Overpayments** endpoints."
61	- "To utilise paging you must append a page query parameter to the URL e.g. `?page=1`."
62	- Page size (exact quote): "The default page size is **100**, with a maximum of **1000** and a minimum of **1**. If users set a value outside the supported range, we automatically adjust it to the nearest supported page size. For example, `?page=1&pageSize=5000` will return 1000 items."
63	- Responses include a pagination object: `"pagination": { "page": 1, "pageSize": 10, "pageCount": 1, "itemCount": 1 }` (supersedes the old "fetch next page until empty" approach).
64	- The Limits page adds: endpoints supporting pagination let you "retrieve line item details for 100 invoices at a time"; Journals are "retrieved only in batches of 100" (https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/limits/).
65	
66	### If-Modified-Since
67	- Exact quote: "specify a UTC timestamp filter using the If-Modified-Since http parameter. Only items created or updated since the specified timestamp will be returned (accurate to the second)."
68	- "We recommend all calls to any endpoints that have large result sets use the if-modified-since parameter."
69	- Caveat (exact quote): "Not all changes will trigger a change of the UpdatedDateUTC field. These include changes to partially paid transactions which don't generate a journal such as DueDate or SentToContact, and Contact fields pulled from other sources such as Balances, IsSupplier, and isCustomer. As a result, transactions with these changes may not be returned with an If-Modified-Since query."
70	
71	### "where" filter
72	- "The where parameter allows you to filter on endpoints and elements that don't have explicit parameters... it must be encoded using percent encoding before it is appended to the URL."
73	- Exact examples from the docs:
74	  1. Strings use double quotes: `Type=="BANK"` → percent encoded: `https://api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0/Accounts?where=Type%3D%3D%22BANK%22`
75	  2. Numbers and dates, `and`/`&&` to connect conditions: `AmountDue > 5000 and DueDate > DateTime(2015, 01, 01)`
76	  3. Date range: `Date >= DateTime(2015, 01, 01) && Date < DateTime(2015, 12, 31)`
77	- Performance warning: "Long, complex where queries can cause time outs... restrict your queries to simple == operations."
78	- Invoices and Contacts also have explicit comma-separated filter params, e.g. `?IDs=...,...`, `?ContactIDs=...&Statuses=AUTHORISED`, `?Statuses=DELETED,VOIDED`.
79	- `summaryOnly=true` on GET Contacts and Invoices returns a lightweight response.
80	
81	### order
82	- "append an order query string to the endpoint URL", e.g. `?order=EmailAddress`; descending: `?order=EmailAddress%20DESC`.
83	- "Secondary ordering, using the ID field, is now applied by default across a range of endpoints – Contacts, Payments, Batch Payments, Credit Notes, Invoices and Bank Transactions... The default order is `UpdatedDateUTC ASC, [ID] ASC`."
84	
85	### summarizeErrors + validation error envelope
86	- "If you plan to submit more than one entity per API call, we recommend that you append the `summarizeErrors=false` querystring... This ensures that every entity is returned back to you, each having their own status attribute." Status per entity: OK, WARNING or ERROR.
87	- "If you have a validation error in any of your objects you will receive a HTTP 200 status code (rather than HTTP 400) if you are using the summarizeErrors parameter."
88	- "The summarizeErrors option is only available for the Accounting API."
89	- Bulk response example (quoted from the page, abridged):
90	  ```json
91	  {
92	    "Invoices": [
93	      { "InvoiceID": "5ceffde5-...", "StatusAttributeString": "OK" },
94	      { "InvoiceID": "5ceffde5-...", "StatusAttributeString": "WARNING",
95	        "Warnings": [{ "Message": "Only AUTHORISED invoices may have SentToContact updated." }] },
96	      { "InvoiceID": "5ceffde5-...", "StatusAttributeString": "ERROR",
97	        "ValidationErrors": [{ "Description": "Invoice not of valid type for creation" }] }
98	    ]
99	  }
100	  ```
101	- HTTP 400 envelope (Elements[].ValidationErrors) — exact example from the Response Codes page (https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/responsecodes): "HTTP 400 responses include an 'ApiException' element in the response that contains a useful summary of the reason for the error."
102	  ```json
103	  {
104	    "ErrorNumber": 10,
105	    "Type": "ValidationException",
106	    "Message": "A validation exception occurred",
107	    "Elements": [{
108	      "ValidationErrors": [{
109	        "Message": "Email address must be valid"
110	      }]
111	    }]
112	  }
113	  ```
114	- PUT vs POST: "A PUT method will create new data in Xero, whereas a POST will either create new data or update existing data in Xero."
115	
116	---
117	
118	## 4. Idempotency
119	
120	Source: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/idempotent-requests/idempotency/
121	(Not mentioned on the three primary pages; this is the official guide, reached via the docs nav "Idempotent Requests".)
122	
123	- **Idempotency-Key header IS supported** (exact quote): "To guarantee idempotency, you need to include the 'Idempotency-Key' header on your requests. Xero will then cache the response to these requests and if subsequent requests are made with the same idempotency key, they won't be processed and instead the cached response will be returned."
124	- Mutating methods only: "Xero will only process idempotency for requests that mutate data, I.e. POST, PUT and PATCH. The idempotency key header will be ignored for all other HTTP methods."
125	- Key lifetime: "keys are stored for **6 minutes** from the time of the first call, after which they expire."
126	- Key reuse with a different request → HTTP 400: `Idempotency Key: KEY_VALUE is used with a different request.` (Scope is per app; a "different request" = any change to endpoint URL, request body, or HTTP method.)
127	- Max key length **128 characters** (over → 400: `Idempotency Key: KEY_VALUE with length KEY_LENGTH is too long.`). Header name is not case-sensitive. No server-side validation of key format — uniqueness is the caller's responsibility. Xero recommends concatenating four UUIDs.
128	- Errors are cached too: "If an idempotent request errors out internally, the error will be cached and returned when the request is re-run even if the internal error is resolved."
129	- Interaction with rate limits: "Idempotency is checked after rate limits have been applied and thus duplicate requests will count towards rate limits and reduce the limit remaining headers."
130	- The guide's example request includes headers `Authorization`, `Accept`, `Xero-tenant-id`, `Idempotency-Key` on `PUT https://api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0/Accounts`.
131	
132	---
133	
134	## Fetch notes
135	- All three requested primary URLs returned HTTP 200 (no 404s) but required JS rendering; WebFetch timed out repeatedly, so pages were rendered in Chrome and text extracted from the live DOM.
136	- Supplementary official pages used (reached via the docs nav): Client Credentials, Token types, Tenants, Idempotent requests, Response Codes — all under developer.xero.com.
137	- Single UNVERIFIED item: an explicit docs sentence stating the `xero-tenant-id` header is/isn't required for Custom Connections (the current page only implies "not required" by omitting it from the canonical call example).
138
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-fable-5 2026-07-04 12:01:23Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.tmp/xero-refs/undefined/style.md`

```text
1	# Xero prompt-library skill-writing style: fact sheet
2	
3	Sources (all in `.tmp/xero-refs/xero-prompt-library/`):
4	- `lovable/xero-integration-single-account/SKILL.md` (707 lines)
5	- `lovable/xero-integration-multi-account/SKILL.md` (868 lines)
6	- `javascript/SKILL.md` (`xero-integration-multi-tenant`, 452 lines)
7	- `python/SKILL.md` (`xero-integration-multi-tenant-python`, 502 lines)
8	- `replit/xero-integration-single-account-skill` (plain file with SKILL.md frontmatter, 293 lines)
9	- `README.md` (repo index, no skill discipline content)
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. Document structure conventions
14	
15	### Frontmatter
16	Exactly two fields, always: `name` and `description`. No version, no tools, no metadata.
17	
18	- `name` is the kebab-case skill id (`xero-integration-single-account`, `xero-integration-multi-tenant-python`).
19	- `description` is a single long paragraph that does four jobs, in roughly this order:
20	  1. **What it builds**, with the discriminating word in CAPS: "Build a MULTI-TENANT Xero integration where EACH end user of the app connects THEIR OWN Xero organisation".
21	  2. **When to use it**: "Use whenever building a multi-tenant SaaS on Xero".
22	  3. **What it covers**, as a comma list of the hard parts: "Covers the full OAuth2 + PKCE lifecycle with the official `xero-node` SDK, per-user encrypted token storage, refresh/rotation, tenant selection, scopes (including the granular-scope migration), and the Xero API gotchas that bite in production (date/money/tax normalisation, idempotent writes, rate limits)."
23	  4. **A read-first command and a routing rule to the sibling skill**: "Read this BEFORE writing any multi-tenant Xero code. If instead the app talks to a SINGLE Xero org that the builder owns, do NOT use this flow — see 'Do you actually need this?' at the top for the much simpler single-tenant approach."
24	
25	  The Replit variant adds an anti-pattern warning right in the description: "Read this before writing any Xero code; do NOT rely on generic xero-node knowledge."
26	
27	### Section order (consistent across all four full skills)
28	1. `# H1` restating the variant in plain words: "Multi-tenant Xero (each user connects their own Xero)".
29	2. **Variant check / decision gate first.** A blockquote or `## Do you actually need this?` section forcing the reader to confirm they picked the right skill, with an explicit pointer to the sibling: "Stop and use **`xero-integration-single-account`** instead." Each pair of skills cross-references the other by exact name. The deciding question is stated as one sentence: "The deciding question is *how many Xero logins exist*".
30	3. **Runtime context** ("Where this code runs in Lovable") — the platform facts that shape all code below, stated as numbered "runtime facts".
31	4. `## Decision check before building` — a 3-item ALL-must-be-true checklist.
32	5. `## ⚠️ Pre-flight — do this FIRST (skipping it costs hours)` — see §2 below.
33	6. Numbered build sections in lifecycle order: `## 1. Register your own Xero app` → `## Scopes` → `## 2. Per-user token store` → `## 3.` (client/redirect resolver) → `## 4.` (start consent) → `## 5.` (callback) → `## 6.` (use per request / status UX).
34	7. Cross-cutting sections: `## Date + money + tax normalisation`, `## Idempotency (avoid duplicate writes)`, `## Other Xero gotchas (these bite in production)`, platform-gotcha subsection, `## Security checklist (do not skip)`, `## Don't want to hand-roll it?` (managed alternatives: Nango, Composio).
35	8. `## Verify` — always last. A numbered end-to-end acceptance list (they even use step `0.` and `0.5.` to force pre-click checks before step 1). Every item is observable behavior, e.g. "Post the same invoice twice with the same idempotency key and confirm only one invoice is created."
36	
37	### Description/heading phrasing
38	- Headings carry the verdict, not just the topic: "The redirect-URI resolver — read carefully", "Other Xero gotchas (these bite in production)", "Security checklist (do not skip)", "CRITICAL: path mapping", "Failure handling (the rule that bites)".
39	- Section intros state *why the section exists* in one or two sentences before any code.
40	- Every code block opens with a file-path comment (`// src/lib/server/crypto.server.ts`) so the reader knows where it lives.
41	- Sync-maintenance notes between sibling docs: "(This table is identical to the single-account skill — keep the two in sync if Xero's assignments change.)"
42	- External source-of-truth links with a staleness warning: "Source of truth (re-check periodically — dates and assignments change): https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/scopes"
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 2. Pre-flight check discipline (verbatim quotes)
47	
48	The Lovable skills open the build with a dedicated section. Header, verbatim:
49	
50	> ## ⚠️ Pre-flight — do this FIRST (skipping it costs hours)
51	>
52	> Three checks. Each one prevents a class of bug that produces a misleading
53	> upstream error from Xero. Do them **before** you wire the Connect button to a
54	> human.
55	
56	**Check 1 — assert every secret non-empty, with the causal chain stated** (verbatim):
57	
58	> ### 1. Assert every secret is set and non-empty
59	>
60	> Empty/missing secrets surface as `unauthorized_client` from Xero (because
61	> `client_id=undefined` got sent), which points away from the real cause. Fail fast
62	> with a clear message inside `xeroStart` **before** building the URL:
63	
64	```ts
65	function requireEnv(name: string) {
66	  const v = process.env[name];
67	  if (!v || v.trim() === "") {
68	    throw new Error(`${name} is empty — set it in Project Secrets and retry.`);
69	  }
70	  return v;
71	}
72	```
73	
74	> Call `requireEnv("XERO_CLIENT_ID")`, `XERO_CLIENT_SECRET`, `XERO_SCOPES`, and
75	> `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` at the top of every handler that uses them.
76	
77	**Check 2 — log the constructed request once, never the secret** (verbatim):
78	
79	> You want a server log you can read when something is wrong, without ever
80	> printing the secret value itself:
81	
82	```ts
83	console.log("[xero] authorize", {
84	  client_id_len: process.env.XERO_CLIENT_ID?.length,
85	  redirect_uri: redirectUri,
86	  scope: process.env.XERO_SCOPES,
87	});
88	```
89	
90	> If the next failure is `unauthorized_client` or `invalid redirect_uri`, this
91	> line tells you *exactly* which value Xero rejected.
92	
93	Note the trick: log `client_id_len` (the length), never the value.
94	
95	**Check 3 — smoke-test before any human clicks** (verbatim):
96	
97	> ### 3. Smoke-test `xeroStart` BEFORE clicking Connect
98	>
99	> Call the server function and inspect the returned URL: `client_id` must be a
100	> non-empty hex string, and `redirect_uri` must **exactly** match one of the URIs
101	> registered in your Xero app (including scheme, host, and path). If either is
102	> wrong, fix it here. Do not proceed to the popup — Xero's errors past this point
103	> are far less useful than the URL you can read right now.
104	
105	**Check 4 — validate key material shape, with the full failure story** (verbatim):
106	
107	> AES-256-GCM requires a **32-byte** key. A 32-character random string is NOT 32
108	> bytes once base64-decoded (it's ~24), and `createCipheriv` throws
109	> `Invalid key length` from deep inside `encrypt()` — which surfaces as an
110	> opaque **500 on the callback**, *after* Xero has already consumed the
111	> single-use `code`. The user sees "something went wrong" with no way to retry
112	> without re-consenting, and no breadcrumb in the logs points at the key.
113	
114	Pattern to copy: each pre-flight check names (a) the invariant, (b) the misleading downstream symptom if skipped, (c) the exact code to assert it, (d) the moment to run it ("before you wire the Connect button to a human"). Boot-time assertion + named error, exactly the CLAUDE.md rule "Secrets in `.env` only, asserted non-empty at boot with named errors."
115	
116	They also make the *failure path itself* testable — Verify step 0.5, verbatim:
117	
118	> 0.5. **Force an encryption failure once.** Temporarily set `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` to
119	>    a 16-char string and click Connect. Confirm `/dashboard` shows
120	>    `?error=callback&detail=…` (with a readable message) rather than a blank
121	>    500 — this proves both the callback try/catch (§4) and the dashboard
122	>    banner (§6) actually wire failures back to the user. Restore the real
123	>    key afterwards.
124	
125	---
126	
127	## 3. Error → real-cause mappings (verbatim — the gold)
128	
129	These docs mostly encode error→cause as prose sentences and blockquote "traps", not literal tables. Every mapping found, copied verbatim with source:
130	
131	**`unauthorized_client` ← empty/missing client_id** (lovable single + multi, Pre-flight §1):
132	
133	> Empty/missing secrets surface as `unauthorized_client` from Xero (because
134	> `client_id=undefined` got sent), which points away from the real cause.
135	
136	**`invalid redirect_uri` / `unauthorized_client` ← three redirect-origin traps** (lovable, §3 header block, verbatim):
137	
138	> ### ⚠️ Three traps, all of which produce `invalid redirect_uri` or `unauthorized_client`
139	>
140	> **Trap A — `getRequest()` / `request.url` on the server returns
141	> `http://localhost:8080`.** Under the Worker runtime the inbound request you
142	> see inside a server function is on the internal dev host, not the public URL.
143	> Never derive `redirect_uri` from it.
144	>
145	> **Trap B — In the Lovable editor, `window.location.origin` is
146	> `https://<project-id>.lovableproject.com`** (the iframe host), which is NOT
147	> a registered URI. Sending it raw produces `invalid redirect_uri` every time.
148	>
149	> **Trap C — A single `XERO_REDIRECT_URI` secret can't cover both preview and
150	> published.** If you set it to the published host the preview breaks (and
151	> vice-versa). Use the resolver below instead.
152	
153	**`unauthorized_client` / invalid-redirect ← port or trailing-slash mismatch** (javascript, Local-dev gotchas):
154	
155	> **Redirect URI must match exactly.** The most common local failure is a port or
156	> trailing-slash mismatch between `XERO_REDIRECT_URI` and what's registered in the
157	> portal. If consent returns `unauthorized_client` or an invalid-redirect error,
158	> this is almost always why. Run the dev server on the same port you registered.
159	
160	**403 ← missing write scope** (lovable multi, Scopes; wrong-secret-shaped failures are scope failures here):
161	
162	> Shipping a read-only token and then calling `POST /Invoices` fails
163	> at runtime with a 403 / insufficient-scope error — and because scopes **cannot be
164	> widened on an existing token**, the only fix is to send every connected customer
165	> back through the whole consent flow. So decide up front: read, write, or both.
166	
167	and the compressed single-account form:
168	
169	> Mismatched scope → 403 at runtime and the only fix is
170	> to send the user through consent again.
171	
172	and the silent-default variant (lovable multi):
173	
174	> **Make the `XERO_SCOPES` fallback in your shared Xero config match your intent** — an
175	> all-`.read` default will silently hand every customer a read-only token if the
176	> secret is missing, and your first write will 403.
177	
178	**Opaque 500 on callback ← wrong-length `TOKEN_ENC_KEY`** (lovable, Pre-flight §4): see §2 above ("`createCipheriv` throws `Invalid key length` … surfaces as an opaque **500 on the callback**, *after* Xero has already consumed the single-use `code`").
179	
180	**`TypeError: checks.state argument is missing` ← client recreated without state** (javascript, §5 callback, verbatim code comment):
181	
182	> // MUST recreate the client with the SAME state you used to build the consent URL.
183	> // `apiCallback` runs openid-client's state check: because the consent URL carries a
184	> // `state` param, the callback client must also know it, or you get the cryptic
185	> // `TypeError: checks.state argument is missing` and every connect fails.
186	
187	**401 `No connection found for replid ...` ← Repl not bound to the connector** (replit, setup checklist):
188	
189	> **Until the Repl is bound,
190	> every live call fails with `401` and a body like
191	> `No connection found for replid ... with connector: xero`.** If you see that
192	> 401, the code is probably fine — the binding is missing.
193	
194	**404 ← friendly alias paths** (replit, CRITICAL: path mapping):
195	
196	> The connector blueprint advertises friendly aliases like `/accounting/contacts` and
197	> `/accounting/invoices` — **these are wrong and return 404.** Use Xero's real
198	> REST paths with PascalCase resource names.
199	
200	> Verified empirically: `/connections` → 200, `/accounting/organisations` → 404,
201	> `/api.xro/2.0/Organisations` → 200.
202	
203	**Blank white page ← Xero consent framed in an iframe** (lovable + javascript):
204	
205	> Xero's login refuses to be framed (`frame-ancestors`), so a same-frame
206	> redirect from the Lovable preview iframe shows a **blank white page** that
207	> looks like the button is broken.
208	
209	**Access token dies in ~30 min, no recovery ← `offline_access` omitted** (all four):
210	
211	> **`offline_access` is mandatory for refresh.** Omit it and you get an access token
212	> that dies in ~30 min with no way to refresh — the user must re-consent.
213	
214	**400 ← same idempotency key, different request** (all four, Idempotency "Rules that bite"):
215	
216	> **Same key + a *different* request → `400` "used with a different request".** A
217	> request differs if the URL, body, or method changes. Persist the key alongside the
218	> record and reuse the *identical* request when retrying.
219	
220	**Cached error ← retrying an errored keyed request** (all four):
221	
222	> **Errors are cached too.** If the keyed request errored internally, retrying with the
223	> *same* key returns the *same cached error* even after the underlying problem is
224	> fixed. To recover, **GET to check whether the resource was actually created**; if
225	> not, create again with a **new** key.
226	
227	**429 ← rate limits, with the recovery** (all four):
228	
229	> **Rate limits (per tenant):** ~60 calls/minute, 5,000/day, plus an app-wide
230	> per-minute cap and ~5 concurrent requests. On **429** read the `Retry-After` header
231	> and back off — multi-tenant apps hit this fast when looping over orgs. Spread/queue
232	> calls per tenant.
233	
234	**Silent wrong-books write ← missing/wrong tenant header** (all four):
235	
236	> **Always send `Xero-Tenant-Id`** on every API call. There is no implicit "current
237	> org." With multiple connected orgs, the wrong id silently writes to the wrong
238	> company's books.
239	
240	**Next refresh fails ← rotated refresh token not persisted** (all four):
241	
242	> **Token lifetimes:** access token ~30 min; refresh token ~60 days and **rotates on
243	> every refresh**. You MUST persist the new refresh token each time or the next
244	> refresh fails. A refresh token also dies if unused for 60 days.
245	
246	**Sibling orgs flip to needs_reconnect ← only one row updated after rotation** (javascript §6, code comment, verbatim):
247	
248	> // CRITICAL multi-tenant trap: when one consent authorized several orgs, they all
249	> // share ONE token set. Refreshing rotates the refresh token and INVALIDATES the
250	> // old one, so updating only THIS row leaves every sibling org holding a dead token
251	> // (they'll wrongly flip to needs_reconnect on their next call). Update ALL rows
252	> // that shared the previous token set — e.g. match on the old ciphertext:
253	
254	**Duplicate contacts ← posting invoices with a contact name** (all four):
255	
256	> **Contacts are not auto-deduplicated by email.** Posting an invoice with a new
257	> contact name creates a NEW contact every time. Upsert yourself: look up by email (or
258	> `ContactID`) via `getContacts` and reuse the id; otherwise you litter the org with
259	> duplicates.
260	
261	The one true error-shaped *table* in the corpus is the Replit **path mapping table** (Purpose | Path) plus its "verified empirically" line — the pattern being: verified request → expected status, wrong path → 404.
262	
263	---
264	
265	## 4. Scope minimalism rules
266	
267	Canonical statement, repeated near-verbatim in all four skills:
268	
269	> Scopes are **additive** — request the minimum needed and re-run the OAuth consent
270	> flow to add more later. You **cannot** widen scope on an existing token; the user
271	> must re-consent. To get a refresh token at all you must include `offline_access`.
272	
273	Supporting rules:
274	
275	- "**Always prefer the granular scopes** — use `accounting.invoices`, NOT `accounting.transactions`." (Broad scopes deprecated: granular assigned to all Web/PKCE apps since March 2026, custom connections since 29 April 2026, broad dead September 2027.)
276	- "**Read vs write — choose deliberately; you can't change it later without re-consent.** `.read` scopes are GET-only."
277	- Lovable adds an agent-behavior rule: "Do not ask the user what scopes to use - set the appropriate scopes for the integration to function."
278	- Replit: "**Scopes** — see the dedicated section below. Request the minimum set for what you're doing".
279	
280	Exact scope strings quoted in the docs:
281	
282	- Read-only dashboard (lovable single):
283	  `openid profile email offline_access accounting.contacts.read accounting.invoices.read accounting.reports.profitandloss.read`
284	- Write / invoicing app ("A typical invoicing SaaS therefore wants", all four):
285	  `openid profile email offline_access accounting.contacts accounting.invoices`
286	- Typical invoice flow (replit):
287	  `offline_access openid email profile accounting.settings.read accounting.invoices accounting.contacts`
288	
289	Scope reference tables they maintain (structure worth copying): (1) identity scopes `offline_access` / `openid` / `profile` / `email`; (2) "Deprecated (broad) → New granular scopes" migration table (`accounting.transactions` → `accounting.invoices`, `accounting.payments`, `accounting.banktransactions`, `accounting.manualjournals`; same for `.read`; `accounting.reports.read` → eight `accounting.reports.*.read` scopes); (3) "Scope | Grants" table mapping each granular scope to the resources it unlocks (`accounting.invoices` → Invoices, CreditNotes, Quotes, PurchaseOrders, RepeatingInvoices, LinkedTransactions, Items; `accounting.payments` → Payments, BatchPayments, Overpayments, Prepayments); (4) "never broad, use as-is" table (`accounting.settings`, `accounting.contacts`, `accounting.attachments`, `accounting.journals.read`, `accounting.budgets.read`). Always footed with the developer.xero.com scopes URL and "re-check periodically — dates and assignments change".
290	
291	---
292	
293	## 5. Token handling rules
294	
295	- **Lifetimes, stated with numbers every time:** access token ~30 min; refresh token ~60 days, **rotates on every refresh**, dies if unused for 60 days. Expiry stored as `expires_at` computed from `expires_in` seconds: `new Date(Date.now() + tokenSet.expires_in * 1000).toISOString()` (JS) / `token["expires_at"]` epoch-seconds dict key (Python).
296	- **Refresh early, not at expiry:** JS refreshes when `expires_at <= now + 60_000` (60-second buffer); Python "Proactively refresh if within ~2 min of expiry" (`expires_at - time.time() < 120`).
297	- **Persist rotation immediately or break:** "tokenSet = await res.json();   // refresh_token ROTATES — must persist". And update **all sibling rows** sharing the old ciphertext (multi-org consents share one token set) — match siblings on the OLD ciphertext.
298	- **No long-lived clients / no caching:** "Make a fresh client per request and `setTokenSet(...)` from the DB. Do not keep a long-lived client — tokens expire and clients are per-user." Replit: "Never cache this — create a fresh instance per request (tokens rotate)."
299	- **Refresh failure is a state, not a retry loop:** "On a failed refresh, **mark the connection broken and prompt re-connect** — do not silently retry forever." Status enum in the schema: `'active' | 'needs_reconnect'`. Surface it in UI with a reconnect CTA; a 60-day-idle token dies silently, so add a scheduled keep-alive refresh, not just reactive detection.
300	- **Storage:** tokens encrypted at rest (AES-256-GCM, key from `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` in the secret store, exactly 32 bytes base64); rotating token lives in the **database (a mutable store), never in a secret**; "never put per-user tokens in env".
301	- **Never-log / never-expose:** log `client_id_len`, not the value ("without ever printing the secret value itself"); "**Never expose tokens to the browser** or any client-readable storage"; "Add `.env` (and any local token dumps) to `.gitignore`; never commit credentials"; status endpoints return "only non-sensitive metadata (never the token)".
302	- **`state` discipline:** random, persisted server-side with a 10-minute expiry, verified on callback, deleted after use — "single-use, replay-proof".
303	
304	---
305	
306	## 6. Tone and format
307	
308	- **Imperative second person, no hedging.** "Do them **before** you wire the Connect button to a human." "Stop and use X instead." "If you reach for `xero-node` or start writing token-refresh code, stop — you are on the wrong path."
309	- **Warnings are structural, not decorative.** The single warning device is `⚠️` in a heading (`## ⚠️ Pre-flight — do this FIRST`, `> ### ⚠️ Three traps...`, `> ### ⚠️ Open consent in a NEW TAB, opened SYNCHRONOUSLY in the click handler`) plus a blockquote body. Bold and CAPS carry emphasis inline: MUST, NOT, NEVER, ONE, THEIR OWN, FIRST, "byte-for-byte", "byte-match".
310	- **Every rule ships its consequence.** Not "include offline_access" but "Omit it and the access token dies in ~30 min with no way to refresh — the user must re-consent." Failure narratives are causal chains told to the end (wrong key → throw deep inside encrypt → opaque 500 → single-use code consumed → user must re-consent → no breadcrumb in logs).
311	- **Named recurring rubrics:** "Rules that bite", "(these bite in production)", "Local-dev gotchas (these bite during testing)", "Gotcha that fools people", "(do not skip)", "(skipping it costs hours)", "CRITICAL multi-tenant trap".
312	- **Tables vs prose:** tables only for enumerable reference data (scopes, scope grants, deprecation mapping, REST paths). Everything judgment-shaped (gotchas, security, idempotency rules) is bold-lead-in bullets: `**Rule.** Consequence and recovery.` Checklists (Decision check, Security checklist, Verify) are lists, with Verify numbered.
313	- **Code is placed, minimal, and commented with intent** — comments mark traps ("// sync!", "// refresh_token ROTATES — must persist", "// REQUIRED — wrong id writes to wrong books"), not narration. File-path comment on line 1 of each block.
314	- **Distrust of upstream errors is a theme:** fail fast locally because "Xero's errors past this point are far less useful than the URL you can read right now."
315	- **Empirical claims are labeled:** "Verified empirically", "**The paths below are verified — trust them, do not re-probe.**", and for unknowns: "probe it **read-only first** rather than guessing".
316	- Punctuation habits: heavy em-dash usage, `→` arrows for flows and mappings (`DRAFT` → `SUBMITTED` → `AUTHORISED`; "Same key + different request → `400`"), backticked literals for every identifier, header, error string, and scope.
317	
318	---
319	
320	## 7. Platform-specific guidance — do NOT copy into a NestJS project
321	
322	Everything below is Lovable/Supabase/Replit plumbing, not Xero discipline. In NestJS: config assertion at boot (ConfigModule + named errors), one Xero module (`api/src/modules/xero/`), Postgres via your ORM, guards instead of RLS.
323	
324	**Lovable / TanStack Start / Cloudflare Workers:**
325	- `createServerFn` server functions; server routes under `src/routes/api/public/*` to "bypass Lovable's published-site auth gate". NestJS controllers are just public or guarded routes.
326	- "Do not use Supabase Edge Functions"; the legacy-port note (Deno Edge Functions, `Deno.env.get`, redeploy after secret change, `verify_jwt = false`, Web Crypto two-part `iv.ciphertext` blob) — all Deno/Supabase-specific.
327	- "Read `process.env.*` **inside** `.handler()`, not at module scope" (Worker per-request env injection). NestJS reads env once at boot — the opposite discipline.
328	- The whole `resolveRedirectUri` host-mapping resolver, `PROJECT_ID`, `id-preview--*.lovable.app` / `project--*.lovable.app` hosts, `*.lovableproject.com` iframe host, `getRequest()` returning `http://localhost:8080`. A NestJS app has one configured redirect URI per environment.
329	- Iframe/popup dance (`window.open("about:blank", "_blank")` synchronously before `await`) — Lovable editor-preview-specific; keep only the generic fact that Xero consent can't be framed.
330	- "Never import `@/integrations/supabase/client.server` at module scope of a `*.functions.ts` or route file" — client-bundle stripping concern, no NestJS equivalent.
331	- Lovable Cloud secrets / `generate_secret` with `length: 44` — keep the underlying rule (base64 of 32 random bytes), drop the tool.
332	
333	**Supabase data layer:**
334	- `supabaseAdmin`, `SUPABASE_URL` / `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`, "Service-role key never goes to the browser".
335	- RLS incantations: `grant all ... to service_role`, `enable row level security`, "INTENTIONALLY no anon/authenticated policies", `auth.users(id)` FKs, `requireSupabaseAuth` middleware, `has_role(userId, 'admin')`.
336	- Supabase client returning Postgres `numeric` as strings.
337	- The unauthenticated-prototype "escape hatch" (warning banner, accepted-risks list) — Lovable demo-flow specific.
338	
339	**Replit connector:**
340	- `@replit/connectors-sdk` proxy pattern, `connectors.proxy("xero", path, ...)`, Repl binding (`proposeIntegration`/`addIntegration`), the `No connection found for replid` 401, redirect URI `https://replit.com/connectors/oauth/callback`, `pnpm --filter @workspace/<server>` install rule, Integrations → Manage reset.
341	
342	**Portable despite appearing in platform sections** (keep these): exact-match redirect URIs registered per environment; state persisted server-side because HTTP steps are separate invocations; callback errors funneled to a user-visible `?error&detail` redirect (never a bare 500, because the OAuth `code` is single-use); `Xero-Tenant-Id` on every call; idempotency keys on writes; `summarizeErrors=false`; `unitdp=4`; the `/Date(ms+offset)/` normaliser; encrypt-at-rest with validated 32-byte key; `needs_reconnect` status + keep-alive refresh; all Xero access isolated in one module.
343
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-fable-5 2026-07-04 12:01:23Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.tmp/xero-refs/auth-toolkit.md`

```text
1	# Fact sheet: xero-agent-toolkit + xero-command-line (auth & API-call patterns)
2	
3	Source repos (local checkouts under `.tmp/xero-refs/`):
4	- `xero-agent-toolkit/` — examples repo. **Contains zero auth code and zero tool definitions of its own.** Every example spawns `@xeroapi/xero-mcp-server@latest` via `npx` and hands it `XERO_CLIENT_ID` / `XERO_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars. The actual client_credentials flow lives in the MCP server (`xero-mcp-server/src/clients/xero-client.ts`, also checked out here), cited below as the delegated path.
5	- `xero-command-line/` — oclif CLI. Uses **PKCE authorization-code OAuth (browser login), NOT client_credentials**. No client secret anywhere.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. client_credentials flow specifics
10	
11	### xero-agent-toolkit itself: none
12	No match for `client_credentials`, `identity.xero.com`, or `connect/token` anywhere in the repo. The README mandates a **Custom Connection** app type:
13	
14	> "Make sure you are create a [Custom Connection](https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/custom-connections) type of app - Web app and PKCE apps will not work with these examples" — `xero-agent-toolkit/README.md:67`
15	
16	Credentials are passed as env vars to the spawned MCP server, e.g. `xero-agent-toolkit/python/langchain/langchain.py:40-48`:
17	
18	```python
19	client = MultiServerMCPClient({
20	    ...
21	        "args": ["-y", "@xeroapi/xero-mcp-server@latest"],
22	        ...
23	            "XERO_CLIENT_ID": os.getenv("XERO_CLIENT_ID"),
24	            "XERO_CLIENT_SECRET": os.getenv("XERO_CLIENT_SECRET"),
25	```
26	
27	Same pattern in `python/google-adk/google-adk.py:30-37`, `python/openai/openai_agents.py:369-376`, `typescript/openai-agents/index.ts:383-391`, `typescript/langchain/index.ts:39-47`, `python/google-adk/cloud-run-invoicing-agent/xero_invoicing_agent/agent.py:9-21`.
28	
29	### Delegated implementation (xero-mcp-server, what the toolkit actually runs)
30	`xero-mcp-server/src/clients/xero-client.ts:159-174` (`requestToken`):
31	
32	- **Token endpoint:** `POST https://identity.xero.com/connect/token`
33	- **Auth:** HTTP **Basic** header, `base64(clientId:clientSecret)` — NOT body params
34	- **Body:** form-encoded string `grant_type=client_credentials&scope=<urlencoded scopes>`
35	- **Headers:** `Authorization: Basic ...`, `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `Accept: application/json`
36	
37	```ts
38	const credentials = Buffer.from(`${this.clientId}:${this.clientSecret}`).toString("base64");
39	const response = await axios.post(
40	  "https://identity.xero.com/connect/token",
41	  `grant_type=client_credentials&scope=${encodeURIComponent(scope)}`,
42	  { headers: {
43	      Authorization: `Basic ${credentials}`,
44	      "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
45	      Accept: "application/json" } },
46	);
47	```
48	
49	**Scopes ARE passed explicitly** for custom connections (contrary to some docs suggesting they can be omitted). Selection logic at `xero-client.ts:127-157`:
50	1. If `XERO_SCOPES` env var set → use it verbatim.
51	2. Else try legacy V1 scope set (`accounting.transactions accounting.contacts accounting.settings accounting.reports.read payroll.settings payroll.employees payroll.timesheets`, lines 82-90).
52	3. On HTTP 400 with `error === "invalid_scope"` → retry with granular V2 set (`accounting.invoices accounting.payments accounting.banktransactions accounting.manualjournals accounting.reports.aged.read accounting.reports.balancesheet.read accounting.reports.profitandloss.read accounting.reports.trialbalance.read accounting.contacts accounting.settings payroll.*`, lines 93-107).
53	
54	Alternative path: if `XERO_CLIENT_BEARER_TOKEN` is set, a `BearerTokenXeroClient` skips token issuance entirely (`xero-client.ts:16, 206-231`).
55	
56	### xero-command-line: PKCE, not client_credentials
57	`xero-command-line/src/lib/oauth.ts:5-7`:
58	
59	```ts
60	const XERO_AUTH_BASE = 'https://login.xero.com/identity'
61	const XERO_TOKEN_BASE = 'https://identity.xero.com'
62	const REDIRECT_URI = 'http://localhost:8742/callback'
63	```
64	
65	- Authorize URL: `https://login.xero.com/identity/connect/authorize` with `code_challenge_method: 'S256'` (`oauth.ts:64-75`).
66	- Token exchange `oauth.ts:134-155`: `POST https://identity.xero.com/connect/token`, form-encoded body `grant_type=authorization_code&client_id=...&code=...&redirect_uri=...&code_verifier=...`. **No Basic auth, no client secret — client_id goes in the body** (public PKCE client).
67	- Scopes: default list at `oauth.ts:12-30` (granular: `accounting.contacts accounting.settings accounting.invoices accounting.payments accounting.banktransactions accounting.manualjournals accounting.reports.*.read accounting.budgets.read accounting.attachments`); `openid profile email offline_access` always prepended (`oauth.ts:10, 58-62`); overridable via `xero login --scope` or `XERO_SCOPES` env (`src/commands/login.ts:25-29`).
68	
69	---
70	
71	## 2. Tenant id handling
72	
73	### Custom connections (MCP server path used by agent-toolkit)
74	The tenant id is **fetched from `GET https://api.xero.com/connections` immediately after every token grant, and the FIRST connection is used** — it is not omitted or left empty. `xero-mcp-server/src/clients/xero-client.ts:176-190`:
75	
76	```ts
77	// Get the tenant ID from the connections endpoint
78	const token = response.data.access_token;
79	const connectionsResponse = await axios.get(
80	  "https://api.xero.com/connections",
81	  { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, Accept: "application/json" } },
82	);
83	if (connectionsResponse.data && connectionsResponse.data.length > 0) {
84	  this.tenantId = connectionsResponse.data[0].tenantId;
85	}
86	```
87	
88	Every tool handler then passes it as the first SDK arg (which xero-node sends as the `xero-tenant-id` header), e.g. `xero-mcp-server/src/handlers/update-xero-invoice.handler.ts:18-22`:
89	
90	```ts
91	await xeroClient.authenticate();
92	... xeroClient.tenantId,
93	```
94	
95	One empty-string fallback exists: `getOrganisations(this.tenantId || "")` at `xero-client.ts:47-49` — i.e. an empty `xero-tenant-id` is tolerated for the org lookup on custom connections, but everywhere else the resolved tenantId is sent.
96	
97	### xero-command-line
98	Tenant resolved once at login from `GET https://api.xero.com/connections` with `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>` (`src/lib/oauth.ts:157-167`); if multiple orgs, user picks one interactively (`oauth.ts:195-204`). The chosen `tenantId` is persisted per profile in the token cache (`src/lib/auth.ts:5-11, 100-106`) and passed as the first argument to every `accountingApi` call, e.g. `src/commands/invoices/list.ts:48-50`:
99	
100	```ts
101	const result = await this.xeroCall(flags, async (xero, tenantId) => {
102	  const response = await xero.accountingApi.getInvoices(tenantId, ...)
103	```
104	
105	---
106	
107	## 3. Token caching / expiry / refresh-on-401
108	
109	### xero-agent-toolkit / MCP server: NO caching
110	`authenticate()` is called by **every handler before every API call** and unconditionally requests a fresh client_credentials token (no expiry check, no cache) — `xero-mcp-server/src/clients/xero-client.ts:195-203`:
111	
112	```ts
113	public async authenticate() {
114	  const tokenResponse = await this.getClientCredentialsToken();
115	  this.setTokenSet({
116	    access_token: tokenResponse.access_token,
117	    expires_in: tokenResponse.expires_in,
118	    token_type: tokenResponse.token_type,
119	  });
120	}
121	```
122	
123	That is one extra token round-trip (plus one `/connections` call) per tool invocation. Do better in our `api/src/modules/xero/`: cache until `expires_in` with a safety buffer.
124	
125	### xero-command-line: encrypted cache + 60s pre-expiry refresh + refresh-on-401
126	- Cache: AES-256-GCM-encrypted tokens in `~/.config/xero-command-line/tokens.json` (`src/lib/auth.ts:25`, `src/lib/crypto.ts:11,121`), key in OS keychain / scrypt passphrase / file.
127	- Expiry buffer — `src/lib/auth.ts:26, 73-75`:
128	
129	```ts
130	const TOKEN_BUFFER_MS = 60_000 // Refresh 60s before expiry
131	export function isTokenExpired(entry: TokenEntry): boolean {
132	  return Date.now() >= entry.expiresAt - TOKEN_BUFFER_MS
133	}
134	```
135	
136	- `expiresAt` derived from `expires_at` (seconds) or `expires_in`, defaulting to 30 min (`auth.ts:87-96`).
137	- Proactive refresh before building the client (`src/lib/xero-client.ts:22-31`), via `grant_type=refresh_token&client_id=...&refresh_token=...` — again no Basic auth (`src/lib/oauth.ts:209-231`).
138	- **Refresh-on-401 retry loop** — `src/lib/xero-client.ts:45-78` (`withRetry`, `maxRetries = 2`):
139	
140	```ts
141	// Handle 401: try refreshing token
142	if (statusCode === 401 && attempt < maxRetries) {
143	  const cached = await getCachedTokenSet(profileName)
144	  if (cached?.refreshToken) {
145	    try {
146	      const newTokenSet = await refreshAccessToken(clientId, cached.refreshToken)
147	      await cacheTokenSet(profileName, newTokenSet, cached.tenantId, cached.tenantName)
148	      continue
149	    } catch {
150	      clearCachedToken(profileName)
151	      throw new Error(`Session expired. Run "xero login" to re-authenticate.`)
152	    }
153	  }
154	  clearCachedToken(profileName)
155	  continue
156	}
157	```
158	
159	---
160	
161	## 4. Tool/command inventory
162	
163	### xero-agent-toolkit
164	Defines **no tools itself** — it exposes whatever `@xeroapi/xero-mcp-server` publishes. The TS examples attach the server unfiltered ("basic Xero agent with all tools", `typescript/openai-agents/index.ts:35-48`); the Google ADK examples filter to subsets via `tool_filter` (`python/google-adk/google-adk.py:40-55`: `list-contacts, create-contact, list-invoices, create-invoice, get-timesheet, list-accounts, list-items, list-tax-rates, list-tracking-categories, update-contact, update-invoice, create-payment, list-payments, list-organisation-details`; `cloud-run-invoicing-agent/xero_invoicing_agent/agent.py:22-31`: 8-tool invoicing subset).
165	
166	Full underlying surface (from `xero-mcp-server/src/tools/{create,delete,get,list,update}/`), 50 tools:
167	- create: `create-bank-transaction, create-contact, create-credit-note, create-invoice, create-item, create-manual-journal, create-payment, create-payroll-timesheet, create-quote, create-tracking-category, create-tracking-options`
168	- list: `list-accounts, list-aged-payables-by-contact, list-aged-receivables-by-contact, list-bank-transactions, list-contact-groups, list-contacts, list-credit-notes, list-invoices, list-items, list-manual-journals, list-organisation-details, list-payments, list-payroll-employee-leave, list-payroll-employee-leave-balances, list-payroll-employee-leave-types, list-payroll-employees, list-payroll-leave-periods, list-payroll-leave-types, list-payroll-timesheets, list-profit-and-loss, list-quotes, list-report-balance-sheet, list-tax-rates, list-tracking-categories, list-trial-balance`
169	- update: `approve-payroll-timesheet, revert-payroll-timesheet, update-bank-transaction, update-contact, update-credit-note, update-invoice, update-item, update-manual-journal, update-payroll-timesheet-add-line, update-payroll-timesheet-update-line, update-quote, update-tracking-category, update-tracking-options`
170	- get: `get-payroll-timesheet`; delete: `delete-payroll-timesheet`
171	
172	**Answers:** purchase order tools: **NO**. Attachment tools: **NO**. History tools: **NO**. Payment tools: **YES** (`create-payment`, `list-payments` — no update/delete payment). The toolkit does NOT cover more than the MCP server; it is strictly a consumer of it. POs/attachments/history must go through our raw Accounting API module as planned.
173	
174	### xero-command-line (from `src/commands/`)
175	`login`, `logout`; `profile add|list|remove|set-default`; `org details`; `contacts list|create|update`; `contact-groups list`; `accounts list|update`; `invoices list|create|update`; `quotes list|create|update`; `credit-notes list|create|update`; `manual-journals list|create|update`; `bank-transactions list|create|update`; `payments list|create`; `items list|create|update`; `currencies list`; `tax-rates list`; `tracking categories list|create|update`; `tracking options create|update`; `reports trial-balance|profit-and-loss|balance-sheet|aged-receivables|aged-payables`.
176	
177	No purchase orders, no attachments, no history endpoints here either (though the default login scope list includes `accounting.attachments`, `oauth.ts:29`).
178	
179	---
180	
181	## 5. Rate limits / retry / Retry-After
182	
183	- **xero-agent-toolkit:** nothing concrete — README marketing lines only (`README.md:98` "Robust error management and retry logic"; `typescript/langchain/README.md:162` mentions "Xero API rate limits" in a troubleshooting list). No code.
184	- **xero-command-line:** `withRetry` in `src/lib/xero-client.ts:81-84` does NOT auto-retry 429s — it surfaces the wait time:
185	
186	```ts
187	if (statusCode === 429) {
188	  const retryAfter = extractRetryAfter(parsed, lastError.message)
189	  throw new Error(`Rate limited. Retry after ${retryAfter} seconds.`)
190	}
191	```
192	
193	  `extractRetryAfter` (`xero-client.ts:159-170`) reads the `retry-after` response header from the parsed SDK error, falls back to regex `/retry-after[":\s]+(\d+)/i` over the error message, defaults to **60 seconds**.
194	- **xero-mcp-server** (delegated): no retry either; maps 429 to a friendly string for the LLM — `src/helpers/format-error.ts:35-36`: `"Too many requests to Xero. Please try again in a moment."`
195	
196	---
197	
198	## 6. SDK vs raw HTTP
199	
200	| Repo | API calls | Token/identity calls |
201	|---|---|---|
202	| xero-agent-toolkit | none directly (MCP server does everything); frameworks: `langchain-mcp-adapters`, `@langchain/mcp-adapters`, `google-adk MCPToolset`, `@openai/agents MCPServerStdio` | none |
203	| xero-mcp-server (delegated) | `xero-node` SDK (`accountingApi.*`, class extends `XeroClient`, `src/clients/xero-client.ts:1-8,23`) | raw **axios** for `connect/token` and `/connections` (`xero-client.ts:164, 178`) |
204	| xero-command-line | `xero-node` **^13** SDK (`package.json`; client built with `new XeroClient({clientId, clientSecret: ''})` + `setTokenSet({access_token})`, `src/lib/xero-client.ts:33-34`) | raw **fetch** for authorize/token/refresh/connections (`src/lib/oauth.ts:143, 158, 219`) |
205	
206	Extra pattern worth copying: the CLI injects a `user-agent: xero-command-line-<version>` default header into the SDK (`src/lib/get-client-headers.ts:3-9`, applied at `src/lib/xero-client.ts:36-40`).
207
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```typescript
// .claude/skills/xero-demo-seed/scripts/preflight.ts
//
// Read-only pre-flight for the demo-org seed. Run BEFORE any seed write.
// Compiles standalone: strict, target=ES2022, module=commonjs, esModuleInterop,
// types=["node"]. Node builtins only. Node 18+ (global fetch).
//
// .env is loaded by the runner (e.g. `node --env-file=.env` on Node 20.6+);
// this script only reads process.env and asserts.
//
// Asserts, in order:
//   1. XERO_CLIENT_ID / XERO_CLIENT_SECRET non-empty (named MissingEnv errors)
//   2. client_credentials token mints and carries the seed scopes (MissingScope)
//   3. GET /Organisations returns 200 — prints the raw body so a human confirms
//      this is the DEMO org before anything is written into it.
//
// Custom Connections are bound to ONE Xero organisation, so there is no
// XERO_TENANT_ID env var and no xero-tenant-id header anywhere below.

const TOKEN_URL = "https://identity.xero.com/connect/token";
const API_BASE = "https://api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0";

// Granular scopes only. accounting.invoices covers Invoices AND PurchaseOrders;
// accounting.contacts covers Contacts. Scopes must ALSO be enabled on the
// Custom Connection app config — they are configured at both ends.
const SEED_SCOPES = "accounting.contacts accounting.invoices";

export interface TokenResponse {
  access_token: string;
  expires_in: number;
  token_type: string;
  scope: string;
}

export function requireEnv(name: string): string {
  const v = process.env[name];
  if (v === undefined || v.trim() === "") {
    throw new Error(
      `MissingEnv: ${name} is empty — this surfaces from Xero as unauthorized_client, ` +
        `which points away from the real cause. Set it in .env and retry.`,
    );
  }
  return v;
}

export async function getAccessToken(): Promise<TokenResponse> {
  const clientId = requireEnv("XERO_CLIENT_ID");
  const clientSecret = requireEnv("XERO_CLIENT_SECRET");

  // Log the shape, never the secret.
  console.log("[seed:preflight] token request", {
    client_id_len: clientId.length,
    scope: SEED_SCOPES,
  });

  const res = await fetch(TOKEN_URL, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: {
      Authorization:
        "Basic " + Buffer.from(`${clientId}:${clientSecret}`).toString("base64"),
      "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
    },
    body: new URLSearchParams({
      grant_type: "client_credentials",
      scope: SEED_SCOPES,
    }).toString(),
  });

  if (!res.ok) {
    const body = await res.text();
    throw new Error(
      `TokenRequestFailed: HTTP ${res.status} from ${TOKEN_URL} — ${body}`,
    );
  }

  const token = (await res.json()) as TokenResponse;

  for (const s of SEED_SCOPES.split(" ")) {
    if (!token.scope.includes(s)) {
      throw new Error(
        `MissingScope: token granted "${token.scope}" but the seed needs "${s}" — ` +
          `enable it on the Custom Connection app config AND send it in the token ` +
          `request body (custom-connection scopes are configured at both ends).`,
      );
    }
  }

  return token;
}

async function main(): Promise<void> {
  const token = await getAccessToken();
  console.log("[seed:preflight] token OK", {
    expires_in: token.expires_in,
    scope: token.scope,
  });

  // Smoke test + org identity check. NO xero-tenant-id header: a custom
  // connection token is already bound to exactly one organisation.
  const res = await fetch(`${API_BASE}/Organisations`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token.access_token}`,
      Accept: "application/json",
    },
  });
  const body = await res.text();
  if (!res.ok) {
    throw new Error(
      `OrgCheckFailed: HTTP ${res.status} from GET /Organisations — ${body}`,
    );
  }

  // Print raw JSON, don't parse: a human must confirm this is the DEMO org
  // (and that the base currency is GBP) before any seed write.
  console.log(
    "[seed:preflight] GET /Organisations 200 — confirm this is the DEMO org before seeding:",
  );
  console.log(body);
}

if (require.main === module) {
  main().catch((err: unknown) => {
    console.error(err);
    process.exitCode = 1;
  });
}

```

```text
File created successfully at: /Users/devansh/hackathonProjects/encode-xero-hack/.claude/skills/xero-demo-seed/scripts/preflight.ts (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
```
